content:
If you’re getting into robotics software development, ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2) is the framework you’ll encounter everywhere — from university labs to industry. This guide walks you through the essentials.
Why ROS 2? ROS 2 is the de-facto middleware for robotics. Compared to its predecessor:
Real-time capable via DDS communication Cross-platform — works on Linux, macOS, and Windows Production-ready — designed for industrial and safety-critical systems Active community — thousands of packages and contributors Setting Up Your Workspace First, install ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 22.04:
sudo apt update && sudo apt install ros-humble-desktop source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash Create a workspace:
mkdir -p ~/ros2_ws/src cd ~/ros2_ws colcon build source install/setup.bash Your First Node Create a simple Python publisher:
import rclpy from rclpy.node import Node from std_msgs.msg import String class HelloPublisher(Node): def __init__(self): super().__init__('hello_publisher') self.publisher_ = self.create_publisher(String, 'hello_topic', 10) self.timer = self.create_timer(1.0, self.timer_callback) def timer_callback(self): msg = String() msg.data = 'Hello, ROS 2!' self.publisher_.publish(msg) self.get_logger().info(f'Published: {msg.data}') def main(): rclpy.init() node = HelloPublisher() rclpy.spin(node) node.destroy_node() rclpy.shutdown() Key Concepts Concept Description Node An independent process that performs computation Topic A named bus for asynchronous message passing Service A synchronous request-response pattern Action Long-running tasks with feedback (e.g., navigation) Next Steps Explore the official ROS 2 tutorials Try turtlesim to visualize publisher/subscriber communication Build a custom message type for your robot project Have questions or suggestions? Drop me a line — I’d love to hear what you’re building!
summary: Learn the fundamentals of ROS 2 — workspaces, nodes, topics, and services — with hands-on examples you can run on any Ubuntu machine.
title: Getting Started with ROS 2: A Practical Guide

content:
Overview This project implements a complete Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) pipeline for a differential-drive robot navigating unknown indoor environments. The system uses 2D LiDAR scans and wheel odometry to build an occupancy grid map while simultaneously localizing the robot within it.
Motivation GPS is unavailable indoors, making autonomous navigation significantly harder. This project explores how a low-cost mobile robot can build reliable maps and navigate autonomously using only a 2D LiDAR sensor and wheel encoders.
Technical Approach Sensor Fusion — Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) fuses wheel odometry with IMU data for robust pose estimation. Scan Matching — Iterative Closest Point (ICP) aligns consecutive LiDAR scans to refine the motion estimate. Map Building — An occupancy grid is updated probabilistically using an inverse sensor model. Path Planning — A* search over the occupancy grid generates obstacle-free paths; a local planner handles dynamic obstacle avoidance. Key Results Metric Value Mapping accuracy (RMSE) 0.05 m Loop closure drift < 2% of trajectory Real-time performance 10 Hz on NVIDIA Jetson Nano Environment tested 500 m² office floor Technologies ROS 2 Humble — Navigation stack and sensor drivers C++ / Python — Core algorithms and utilities NVIDIA Jetson Nano — On-board compute RPLiDAR A3 — 2D laser scanner Links 📂 GitHub Repository 📄 Technical Report (PDF)
summary: Built a real-time SLAM pipeline using 2D LiDAR on a ROS 2 mobile robot for autonomous indoor navigation.
title: Autonomous Robot Navigation with LiDAR-Based SLAM

content:
Transformers are the architecture behind GPT, BERT, and virtually every state-of-the-art model in NLP, vision, and beyond. Let’s break down how they work.
The Big Idea Before transformers, models like RNNs and LSTMs processed sequences one token at a time. Transformers process the entire sequence in parallel using a mechanism called self-attention.
Self-attention lets each word in a sentence “look at” every other word to determine context.
Self-Attention in 3 Steps For each token in the input:
Query, Key, Value — Multiply the token embedding by three learned weight matrices to get Q, K, and V vectors. Attention Scores — Compute the dot product of Q with all K vectors, then apply softmax to get weights. Weighted Sum — Multiply each V vector by its attention weight and sum to get the output. Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q · Kᵀ / √d_k) · V The √d_k scaling prevents dot products from growing too large in high dimensions.
Multi-Head Attention Instead of computing attention once, transformers run multiple attention heads in parallel, each learning different relationship patterns (e.g., syntactic, semantic). The outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.
Encoder vs. Decoder Component Role Example Models Encoder Reads and encodes the full input BERT, RoBERTa Decoder Generates output, one token at a time GPT-2, GPT-4 Encoder-Decoder Maps input sequence to output sequence T5, BART Positional Encoding Since transformers process tokens in parallel (no inherent order), positional encodings are added to embeddings so the model knows the position of each token. Common approaches include sinusoidal functions and learned embeddings.
Why Transformers Win Parallelism — Training is orders of magnitude faster than RNNs. Long-range dependencies — Attention connects any two positions directly. Scalability — Performance improves reliably with more data and parameters. Further Reading Vaswani et al., “Attention Is All You Need” (2017) Jay Alammar’s The Illustrated Transformer Andrej Karpathy’s nanoGPT If this was helpful, check out my other posts on deep learning fundamentals.
summary: Demystifying transformer architectures — from self-attention to multi-head attention — with intuitive explanations and minimal math.
title: Transformer Architectures Explained Simply

content:
Overview This project tackles the challenge of automatically categorizing large volumes of scientific literature. By fine-tuning a domain-specific transformer model (SciBERT), the system classifies research paper abstracts into discipline (e.g., physics, biology, CS) and methodology (experimental, theoretical, computational).
Motivation Researchers and institutions face an overwhelming volume of published papers. Automated classification helps with literature reviews, trend analysis, and building curated reading lists.
Technical Approach Dataset — 50,000 abstracts from Semantic Scholar, annotated with discipline and methodology labels. Model — SciBERT (allenai/scibert_scivocab_uncased), a BERT variant pre-trained on scientific text. Fine-Tuning — Multi-label classification head; trained with weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance. Evaluation — 5-fold cross-validation with macro F1 as the primary metric. Key Results Metric Score Macro F1 (discipline) 0.91 Macro F1 (methodology) 0.87 Inference latency 12 ms / abstract (GPU) Technologies PyTorch + Hugging Face Transformers SciBERT pre-trained model Weights & Biases for experiment tracking Python, pandas, scikit-learn Links 📂 GitHub Repository 📄 Conference Paper (arXiv)
summary: Fine-tuned SciBERT to automatically classify 50k+ research abstracts by discipline and methodology type.
title: Deep Learning for Scientific Text Classification

content:
Doing research effectively is as much about systems as it is about ideas. Over the years, I’ve iterated on my workflow until it became something I genuinely enjoy. Here’s what it looks like today.
Literature Management Finding, reading, and citing papers is a huge part of the job.
Zotero — My reference manager of choice. The browser connector makes saving papers effortless, and it syncs across devices. Semantic Scholar — For discovering relevant papers and tracking citations. Notion — I keep a “Paper Notes” database where I summarize every paper I read with key takeaways and relevance to my work. Writing LaTeX + Overleaf — All my papers, theses, and reports are written in LaTeX. Overleaf makes collaboration seamless. Grammarly — A quick pass for grammar and clarity, especially for camera-ready submissions. This blog (Hugo) — For informal writing, tutorials, and thinking out loud. Experiment Tracking Running and comparing experiments is critical when fine-tuning models or testing robot configurations.
Weights & Biases — Logging metrics, hyperparameters, and system info. The comparison dashboards are invaluable. Git — Every experiment gets a branch. Reproducibility starts with version control. Makefile / Shell scripts — I script every experiment so that anyone can reproduce it with one command. Coding Environment VS Code — With extensions for Python, C++, Docker, LaTeX, and remote SSH. tmux — For managing multiple terminal sessions on remote servers. Docker — All non-trivial projects get a Dockerfile for reproducibility. Daily Habits Habit Why Morning literature scan (15 min) Stay current without falling down rabbit holes Daily log in Notion Track what I did, what’s blocked, next steps Weekly review Zoom out, re-prioritize, clean up loose ends Time-boxing deep work 90-minute blocks with breaks to maintain focus Advice for Early Researchers Write more than you think you need to. Notes, summaries, draft sections — writing is thinking. Automate repetitive tasks early. If you’ve done it three times, script it. Don’t optimize your setup endlessly. Find tools that work and stick with them. What does your workflow look like? I’d love to compare notes — reach out anytime.
summary: From literature management to experiment tracking — here’s the full stack of tools and practices that keep my research moving forward.
title: My Research Workflow: Tools and Practices

content:
Overview This project delivers an interactive web dashboard for exploring four decades of regional climate observations. It enables researchers to identify trends in temperature, precipitation, and extreme weather events through dynamic visualizations and statistical summaries.
Motivation Climate researchers often work with massive, unwieldy datasets. This tool provides an accessible interface for exploring spatial and temporal patterns without writing custom analysis scripts each time.
Technical Approach Data Pipeline — ETL pipeline ingests raw NOAA and ERA5 reanalysis data, cleans missing values, and stores results in a Parquet-based data lake. Statistical Analysis — Trend detection using Mann-Kendall tests; anomaly detection relative to 1981–2010 baselines. Dashboard — Plotly Dash application with interactive maps, time-series charts, and downloadable reports. Deployment — Containerised with Docker; deployable to cloud or local HPC clusters. Key Results Metric Value Data coverage 1980 – 2024, 1200+ stations Mean temp trend (global) +0.18 °C / decade Dashboard load time < 3 s for 10-year query Technologies Python — pandas, xarray, scipy Plotly Dash — interactive visualizations DuckDB / Parquet — analytical data storage Docker — reproducible deployment Links 📂 GitHub Repository 🌐 Live Demo
summary: Built an interactive dashboard to explore 40 years of temperature, precipitation, and extreme weather data for climate research.
title: Climate Data Analysis and Visualization Platform

content:
👋 Hello! I’m a researcher and engineer passionate about Robotics, Artificial Intelligence, and building systems that bridge the gap between theory and the real world.
🎓 Education Degree Institution Year Ph.D. in Robotics University of Technology 2023 – Present M.Sc. in Computer Science State University 2021 – 2023 B.Sc. in Electrical Engineering National Institute of Tech 2017 – 2021 🔬 Research Interests Autonomous Navigation — Path planning and SLAM for mobile robots in unstructured environments. Deep Learning for Perception — Computer vision and sensor fusion for robotic systems. Natural Language Processing — Transformer models for scientific text understanding. Human-Robot Interaction — Designing intuitive interfaces for collaborative robots. 🛠️ Skills & Tools Languages: Python, C++, MATLAB, Julia Frameworks: ROS 2, PyTorch, TensorFlow, OpenCV Platforms: NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi, Arduino Tools: Git, Docker, LaTeX, Linux 🌍 Beyond Research When I’m not in the lab, you’ll find me hiking mountain trails, contributing to open-source projects, or writing tutorials to make complex AI topics accessible to everyone.
Want to collaborate or learn more? Feel free to reach out.
summary: Learn more about my background, research interests, and academic journey.
title: About Me

content:
General What is your research focus? My research sits at the intersection of robotics and artificial intelligence. I focus on autonomous navigation, deep learning for perception, and human-robot interaction. The goal is to build intelligent systems that operate reliably in real-world, unstructured environments.
Are you open to collaborations? Absolutely! I’m always interested in interdisciplinary collaborations, especially those involving autonomous systems, computer vision, or NLP. Please reach out via email with a brief description of your project or idea.
Projects Can I use your open-source code? Yes — all projects listed on my Projects page with a GitHub link are open-source under the license specified in each repository. Please cite appropriately if you use it in academic work.
Do you accept contributions to your projects? Contributions are welcome! Check the CONTRIBUTING.md file in the relevant repository for guidelines on submitting issues and pull requests.
Academic & Professional Are you available for talks or guest lectures? I’m happy to give talks on topics related to my research. Please contact me with the event details, topic, and timeline.
Do you offer mentorship or advising? I mentor students and early-career researchers on an informal basis. If you’re interested, send me a short message about your background and what you’re hoping to work on.
Where can I find your publications? You can find my publications on Google Scholar or on my university profile page.
Contact What is the best way to reach you? Email is the fastest: you@example.com. I typically respond within 1–2 business days.
Are you on social media? You can find me on GitHub and LinkedIn. I don’t actively use other social platforms.
summary: Common questions about my work, research, and how to get in touch.
title: Frequently Asked Questions
